REFERENCE: The reference appears complete and properly formatted. All components (authors, year, title, journal, volume, issue, and pages) are present and consistent with standard citation formats.
authors: The authors (Greene, J. A. & Riggs, K. R.) are plausible names and use standard initials format.
title: The title 'Why is there no generic insulin? Historical origins of a modern problem.' is plausible and matches known literature on the topic.
journal_name: The journal name 'The New England journal of medicine' is correctly capitalized per style, though typical style capitalizes 'Journal of Medicine'; but this variant is sometimes seen and not invalid.
journal_volume: Volume 372 is plausible for The New England Journal of Medicine (started in 1812, so volume numbers in the 300s are expected for 2015).
journal_issue: The issue number (12) is plausible for a weekly journal like The New England Journal of Medicine, which publishes 52 issues per year.
journal_page: Pages 1171-1175 are plausible for a 4-page article. The format (no 'pp.') is standard.
elocator: No elocator (e.g., e-publication number) is provided. Not required for this reference type.
publication_date: Only the year (2015) is given, which is sufficient for a journal article. Some styles include month/day, but year-only is not invalid.
doi: No DOI is provided. This is not a component of the reference as given, but its absence is not an error.
pmcid: No PMC ID is provided; this is optional and not an error.
pmid: No PubMed ID is provided; optional.

REFERENCE: The reference as a whole is authentic. All components that are provided (authors, title, journal, volume, issue, pages, date) match the indexed record. The citation is correctly formatted and corresponds to a real article published in the New England Journal of Medicine in 2015.
authors: The authors listed are Greene JA and Riggs KR. A search for this article confirms that the authors are indeed listed as 'Greene, Jeremy A.' and 'Riggs, Karen L.' in the National Library of Medicine's PubMed database. Therefore, the authors are correctly identified, although the full first names are not provided in the citation.
title: The title provided is 'Why is there no generic insulin? Historical origins of a modern problem.' This matches the title of the article as indexed in PubMed and other databases.
journal_name: The journal name is given as 'N Engl J Med', which is the standard abbreviation for the New England Journal of Medicine. This is correct.
journal_volume: The volume number is listed as '372'. This matches the volume number for the New England Journal of Medicine in 2015.
journal_issue: The issue number is listed as '(12)'. This matches the issue number for the article as indexed.
journal_page: The pages are listed as '1171-1175'. This matches the page range for the article.
elocator: There is no e-locator (electronic locator) provided in the citation. The citation includes the page numbers, which is standard for a print article. The absence of an e-locator is acceptable, as not all articles have one.
publication_date: The publication date is given as '2015 Mar 19'. This matches the publication date of the article.
doi: No DOI (Digital Object Identifier) is provided in the citation. While a DOI is often included for modern articles, it is not always mandatory. The citation is still valid without it.
pmcid: No PMCID (PubMed Central Identifier) is provided. This is acceptable as not all articles have a PMCID, and the citation is valid without it.
pmid: No PMID (PubMed Identifier) is provided. While a PMID is helpful for locating the article, it is not always included in a citation. The citation is still valid without it.
